questTitle: P6 Math Revision: Combined Concepts & Advanced Problems (Part 2)
subject: Mathematics
description: Challenge yourself with advanced percentage, ratio and fraction problems! This worksheet covers combined concepts, three-way ratios, savings problems, event attendance, and spending rate problems. Read each question carefully.

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Three-Way Ratio with Percentages
questions.question: The ratio of Lisa's sweets to Amy's sweets is 2 : 5. Together they have 7 parts. 30% of their combined sweets equals 60% of Ken's sweets. What is 30% as a fraction?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Three-Way Ratio with Percentages
questions.question: Lisa and Amy have 7 parts combined. 30% of their total (which is 3/10 × 7 parts) equals how many parts?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Three-Way Ratio with Percentages
questions.question: 2.1 parts represents 60% of Ken's sweets. If 60% of Ken's sweets is 2.1 parts, how many parts represent 100% of Ken's sweets?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Three-Way Ratio with Percentages
questions.question: Lisa has 2 parts, Amy has 5 parts, and Ken has 3.5 parts. To express these as a ratio with whole numbers, we multiply all by 2. What is the ratio of Lisa : Amy : Ken?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Three-Way Ratio with Percentages - Test Question
questions.question: 60% of Peter's marbles equals 40% of Quinn's marbles. 25% of Peter and Quinn's combined marbles equals 50% of Ryan's marbles. What is the ratio of Peter : Quinn : Ryan?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Multi-Step Problem with Cards
questions.question: In a room with boys, girls and adults: 3/5 are children, 40% of children are boys. Boys are 6/25 of the total. Girls make up what percentage of children?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Multi-Step Problem with Cards
questions.question: Children are 3/5 of total. Girls are 60% of children. What fraction of the total are girls?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Multi-Step Problem with Cards
questions.question: Boys = 6/25, Girls = 9/25, Adults = 2/5 = 10/25. If the total is 25 people, how many adults are there?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Multi-Step Problem with Cards
questions.question: Each girl makes 3 cards, each boy makes 5 cards, each adult makes 10 cards. With 6 boys, 9 girls, and 10 adults (25 people total), how many cards are made?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Multi-Step Problem with Cards - Test Question
questions.question: At a workshop, 2/3 of the participants are students and the rest are mentors. 25% of the students are boys and the rest are girls. Each boy completes 4 tasks, each girl completes 6 tasks, and each mentor completes 8 tasks. If 440 tasks were completed in total, how many mentors were there?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Savings and Spending
questions.question: John saves 30% of his allowance each month. What fraction of his allowance does he save?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Savings and Spending
questions.question: John saves 3/10 of his allowance. What fraction of his allowance does he have left to spend?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Savings and Spending
questions.question: John has 7/10 of his allowance left after saving. He spends 1/2 of this remainder on food. What fraction of his TOTAL allowance does he spend on food?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Savings and Spending
questions.question: If John spends $84 on food, and this represents 7/20 of his total allowance, how would you calculate his total allowance?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: Savings and Spending - Test Question
questions.question: Karen saves 40% of her monthly salary. She spends 2/3 of the remainder on rent and the rest on other expenses. If she spends $600 on other expenses, what is her monthly salary?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Event Attendance
questions.question: At a school event, the ratio of teachers to parents to students is 1 : 4 : 15. How many total parts represent all the people?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Event Attendance
questions.question: Students make up 15 parts out of 20 total parts. What fraction of the attendees are students?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Event Attendance
questions.question: 60% of the students are girls. If students represent 15 parts, how many parts represent girl students?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Event Attendance
questions.question: Girl students = 9 parts, Boy students = 6 parts. The difference is 3 parts. If there are 18 more girl students than boy students, what is the value of 1 part?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Event Attendance - Test Question
questions.question: At a concert, the ratio of adults to teenagers to children is 2 : 5 : 8. 75% of the teenagers are girls. If there are 30 more girl teenagers than boy teenagers, how many people attended the concert in total?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Spending Rates - Setting Up
questions.question: Ali spends $2 per day and Ben spends $5 per day. They start spending on the same day. If Ben has less money than Ali, who will run out of money first?
options: They run out at the same time

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Spending Rates - Setting Up
questions.question: Ben spends $5 per day and runs out of money after a certain number of days. How would you calculate the total amount Ben had?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Spending Rates - Setting Up
questions.question: In Case 1: Ali spends $2/day, Ben spends $5/day. In Case 2: Ali spends $5/day, Ben spends $2/day. Ben has the same total money in both cases. If Ben takes D₁ days in Case 1 and D₂ days in Case 2, what is the ratio D₁ : D₂?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Spending Rates - Setting Up
questions.question: The ratio of days is 2 : 5. If Ali spends $2/day for 2 units of days vs $5/day for 5 units of days, what is the ratio of Ali's total spending (Case 1 : Case 2)?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Spending Rates - Setting Up - Test Question
questions.question: Chris and Dan have some money. If Chris spends $3 per day and Dan spends $7 per day, when Dan finishes, Chris has $45 left. If Chris spends $7 per day and Dan spends $3 per day, when Dan finishes, Chris has $5 left. How much money did Dan have?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Spending Rates - Solving
questions.question: Ali's spending in Case 1 is 4 parts, and in Case 2 is 25 parts. Ali has $62 left in Case 1 and $20 left in Case 2. What is the relationship between Ali's spending and remainder?
options: Ali's spending + remainder = constant (Ali's starting amount)
questions.answer: Ali's spending + remainder = constant (Ali's starting amount)

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Spending Rates - Solving
questions.question: Case 1: Spending = 4 parts, Left = $62. Case 2: Spending = 25 parts, Left = $20. The difference in spending (25 - 4 = 21 parts) equals the difference in remainder ($62 - $20 = $42). What is the value of 1 part?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Spending Rates - Solving
questions.question: If 1 part = $2, and Ali's spending in Case 1 is 4 parts, how much did Ali spend in Case 1?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Spending Rates - Solving
questions.question: Ali spent $8 in Case 1 and had $62 left. How much money did Ali have at the start?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Spending Rates - Solving - Test Question
questions.question: Emma and Fiona have some money. If Emma spends $4 per day and Fiona spends $6 per day, when Fiona finishes, Emma has $52 left. If Emma spends $6 per day and Fiona spends $4 per day, when Fiona finishes, Emma has $28 left. How much money did Fiona have?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Percentage Change Problems
questions.question: A shop increases the price of an item by 20%. The new price is $60. What was the original price?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Percentage Change Problems
questions.question: If an item's price is increased by 20%, the new price represents what percentage of the original price?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Percentage Change Problems
questions.question: A price is first increased by 25%, then decreased by 20%. Is the final price the same as the original price?
options: No, the final price is higher than the original
No, the final price is lower than the original
questions.answer: No, the final price is higher than the original

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Percentage Change Problems
questions.question: An item costs $100. It is increased by 25% to become $125. Then it is decreased by 20%. What is the final price?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Percentage Change Problems - Test Question
questions.question: The price of a bag was increased by 30%. During a sale, the new price was decreased by 20%. If the final price is $52, what was the original price of the bag?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Mixed Review - Combined Concepts
questions.question: The ratio of red beads to blue beads is 3 : 7. If 20% of all the beads are removed (keeping the ratio the same), what fraction of the original beads remain?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Mixed Review - Combined Concepts
questions.question: 40% of the boys and 25% of the girls in a class wear glasses. If there are equal numbers of boys and girls, what percentage of the whole class wears glasses?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Mixed Review - Combined Concepts
questions.question: Shop A sells a book for $40 after a 20% discount. Shop B sells the same book for $42 after a 30% discount. Which shop has the higher original price?
options: Both have the same original price

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Mixed Review - Combined Concepts
questions.question: The ratio of Amy's money to Ben's money is 5 : 3. Amy gives 25% of her money to Ben. What is the new ratio of Amy's money to Ben's money?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Mixed Review - Combined Concepts - Test Question
questions.question: In a school, 60% of the students are girls. 25% of the girls and 40% of the boys participate in sports. If 156 students participate in sports, how many students are there in the school?
